original_abstract: In October 2006, the American Council on Education published an issue brief titled *Students on the Move: The Future of International Students in the United States*. This report synthesizes current research on international student mobility and evaluates the competitive standing of the U.S. against other major host nations. The findings reveal that while the United States remains the primary destination for global talent, its traditional dominance is being challenged. Shifts in the market share of established host countries, the emergence of new regional education hubs, and the rising capacity of "sender" nations like China and India to retain their own students suggest that the U.S. can no longer take its leading position for granted in an increasingly competitive global landscape.
humanized_abstract: The American Council on Education released an issue brief on 31 October 2006 entitled Students on the Move: The Future of International Students in the United States. The issue brief was a synthesis of research conducted on international student mobility, and attempted to quantify and analyse the competitive position of the United States as an educational destination. Based on the literature review the US is confirmed as the primary destination for students coming overseas to study, although its historical supremacy is said to be under threat. Other destination countries have started to erode the preeminence of the United States in terms of the number of students coming in — Brazil and France, in particular — and in the longer term the rise of international education centres will put further pressure on US educational interests. Other concerns cited by the authors of the issue brief are that of "sender" countries increasing the education quality at home to reduce the need for students to move overseas — China and India, for example. The report on the whole suggests that although the US has held first place so far it may be about to loose that to other destinations in the world.
